metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Physics 11 is the foundational senior physics course, developing students' understanding of motion, forces, energy, and mechanical waves. Students apply mathematical and graphical methods to analyze physical phenomena, conduct quantitative investigations, and develop the problem-solving skills foundational to engineering and post-secondary physics. First Peoples knowledge of natural phenomena and local applications are integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: A foundational senior physics course covering kinematics, dynamics, energy, electricity, and mechanical waves — developing mathematical problem-solving skills and quantitative reasoning for physics and engineering pathways.

big_ideas.statement: An object's motion can be predicted, analyzed, and described.
big_ideas.elaboration: Kinematics provides the mathematical tools to describe and predict how objects move — using position, velocity, acceleration, and time — in one and two dimensions, including projectile motion.

big_ideas.statement: Forces influence the motion of an object.
big_ideas.elaboration: Newton's laws of motion describe how forces — including gravity, friction, normal, tension, and spring forces — interact to change or maintain an object's state of motion. Understanding balanced and unbalanced forces is central to physics and engineering.

big_ideas.statement: Energy is found in different forms, is conserved, and has the ability to do work.
big_ideas.elaboration: The law of conservation of energy governs all physical processes. Work, power, efficiency, and simple machines are all applications of the relationship between force and energy in physical systems.

big_ideas.statement: Mechanical waves transfer energy but not matter.
big_ideas.elaboration: Waves — including sound and other mechanical disturbances — transfer energy through a medium without transporting the medium itself. Understanding wave properties, behaviour, and resonance has broad applications from music to earthquake engineering.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity; make observations to identify questions about natural phenomena; formulate multiple hypotheses and predict quantitative outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can identify and formulate complex questions about motion, forces, energy, and waves.
I can make quantitative observations and generate testable hypotheses with specific predicted values.
I can predict the outcome of a physics investigation based on prior knowledge and physical principles.

competencies.raw: Select appropriate methods; collect reliable quantitative data; use SI units; apply concepts of accuracy, precision, and significant figures.
i_can_statements: I can design and plan a physics investigation selecting appropriate methods, tools, and controlled variables.
I can collect accurate and precise quantitative data using appropriate SI units and measuring instruments.
I can apply significant figures, scientific notation, and error analysis to physics data.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Identify patterns; construct graphs and diagrams; draw evidence-based conclusions; analyze cause-and-effect relationships using mathematical models.
i_can_statements: I can identify patterns in quantitative data sets and represent them using graphs and equations.
I can construct and interpret motion graphs (position-time, velocity-time, acceleration-time).
I can draw conclusions consistent with physical laws and analyze cause-and-effect relationships mathematically.

competencies.raw: Assess methods for sources of error; improve investigations; evaluate validity of models; critically analyze information sources.
i_can_statements: I can identify specific sources of systematic and random error in a physics investigation.
I can evaluate the validity of physical models (e.g., frictionless surface, point mass) and their limitations.
I can suggest concrete procedural improvements to increase the reliability of quantitative results.
I can critically evaluate physics claims in media and scientific sources.

competencies.raw: Design solutions to real-world problems; implement multiple problem-solving strategies; consider the role of scientists in innovation.
i_can_statements: I can apply physics principles to solve real-world engineering and design problems.
I can implement multiple problem-solving strategies including graphical, algebraic, and vector methods.
I can consider how physics innovation addresses local and global challenges.

competencies.raw: Formulate theoretical models; communicate scientific ideas with evidence-based arguments using appropriate mathematical language.
i_can_statements: I can formulate and explain physical models using mathematical equations and diagrams.
I can communicate physics concepts and problem solutions clearly using correct units, notation, and vocabulary.
I can write lab reports that present quantitative results and evidence-based conclusions.

areas.name: Kinematics
areas.raw: An object's motion can be predicted, analyzed, and described.
topics: Vector and scalar quantities: displacement, distance, velocity, speed, acceleration
Uniform motion: position-time and velocity-time graphs
Uniformly accelerated motion: equations of kinematics (v = v₀ + at, d = v₀t + ½at², etc.)
Graphical analysis: slope, area under curves, and interpreting motion graphs
Projectile motion: horizontal and vertical components, range, and max height
Relative motion and reference frames

areas.name: Dynamics
areas.raw: Forces influence the motion of an object.
topics: Contact forces: normal force, friction (static and kinetic), tension, spring force
Free body diagrams: representing all forces on an object
Newton's First Law: inertia and equilibrium
Newton's Second Law: F = ma and net force calculations
Newton's Third Law: action-reaction pairs
Balanced and unbalanced forces: applications to systems on inclines, pulleys, and connected masses

areas.name: Energy and Work
areas.raw: Energy is found in different forms, is conserved, and has the ability to do work.
topics: Work: W = Fd cosθ and the work-energy theorem
Gravitational potential energy: Ep = mgh
Kinetic energy: Ek = ½mv²
Conservation of mechanical energy: solving problems without friction and with friction
Power: P = W/t = Fv
Efficiency: useful output energy vs. total input energy
Simple machines: levers, pulleys, inclined planes, mechanical advantage
DC electric circuits: Ohm's law (V = IR), series and parallel circuits, Kirchhoff's laws
Thermal energy and specific heat capacity: Q = mcΔT

areas.name: Mechanical Waves
areas.raw: Mechanical waves transfer energy but not matter.
topics: Wave generation and propagation: transverse and longitudinal waves
Wave properties: amplitude, wavelength, frequency, period, wave speed (v = fλ)
Wave behaviours: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference
Standing waves and resonance: harmonics and nodes
Sound as a longitudinal wave: compression and rarefaction
Sound characteristics: pitch, loudness, timbre, the decibel scale
Resonance applications: musical instruments, architectural acoustics
Graphical methods in wave analysis
